title: L2: Costumes & Movement
description: Build a walking, animating character using two threads — movement stays smooth while animation runs on its own timer.

steps.title: What are we building?
steps.content:
Costumes & Movement 🎮

In this lesson you will build a character that moves with the arrow keys and animates through its costumes — both happening at the same time, without either slowing the other down.

The trick is threads. Each thread is an independent block of code. A wait() in the animation thread never blocks movement because they run completely separately.

Thread 1 — movement
def game_start():
    set_rotation_style("left-right")
    while True:
        if key_pressed("right"):
            change_x(5)
            point_in_direction(90)
        if key_pressed("left"):
            change_x(-5)
            point_in_direction(-90)
        if_on_edge_bounce()
Thread 2 — animation
def game_start():
    while True:
        if key_pressed("right") or \
           key_pressed("left"):
            next_costume()
        else:
            set_costume(1)
        wait(0.1)

⚠️ Make sure your sprite has at least two costumes before running. Open the Costumes tab in TurboWarp to check — you can import or draw them there.

steps.title: Why Use Two Threads?
steps.content:
Why use two threads? 🤔

Click through to see why putting movement and animation in one loop causes problems, and how separate threads solve it.

steps.title: Build Your Game
steps.content:
Build it yourself 🛠️

Follow the steps below. Use the ← → arrow keys or the buttons to move through them.

💡 Make sure your sprite has at least two costumes first — check the Costumes tab.

PyScratchLesson.createStepper({containerId:'ps-m-build',height:540,defaultHide:['sprite-panel','backdrop-panel','add-sprite'],steps:[{title:'Step 1 — The Threads panel',text:'The Threads panel on the left lists all your code blocks. Each thread runs independently. You will have two threads — one for movement and one for animation.',interactive:false,highlight:'threads',highlightLabel:'Threads panel'},{title:'Step 2 — Set up your threads',text:'Rename the existing thread to movement by clicking the ✏️ pencil icon next to it. Then click + to add a second thread and name it animation.',interactive:true,highlight:'threads',highlightLabel:'Rename + click + here',requires:{threads:['movement','animation']},requiresHint:'Rename your threads to exactly "movement" and "animation" before continuing.'},{title:'Step 3 — The movement code',text:'Click the movement thread to select it. Here is the code to type in the next step:

def game_start():
    set_rotation_style("left-right")
    while True:
        if key_pressed("right"):
            change_x(5)
            point_in_direction(90)
        if key_pressed("left"):
            change_x(-5)
            point_in_direction(-90)
        if_on_edge_bounce()

No wait() here — this thread runs every frame.',interactive:false,highlight:'editor',highlightLabel:'Type here next'},{title:'Step 4 — Type the movement code',text:'Select your movement thread and type the code shown in the previous step.

💡 Press Tab to indent. Press Enter after a colon for auto-indent.',interactive:true,highlight:'editor',requires:{codeContains:{movement:['change_x','key_pressed','if_on_edge_bounce']}},requiresHint:'Your movement thread needs change_x(), key_pressed(), and if_on_edge_bounce() — check the code from Step 3.'},{title:'Step 5 — The animation code',text:'Now click the animation thread to select it. Here is its code:

def game_start():
    while True:
        if key_pressed("right") or key_pressed("left"):
            next_costume()
        else:
            set_costume(1)
        wait(0.1)

The animation only advances when a key is held — the sprite shows an idle pose otherwise. wait(0.1) only slows this thread.',interactive:false,highlight:'threads',highlightLabel:'Click animation thread'},{title:'Step 6 — Type the animation code',text:'Select your animation thread and type the animation code. The wait(0.1) controls how fast costumes cycle — it does not affect movement.',interactive:true,highlight:'editor',requires:{codeContains:{animation:['next_costume','key_pressed','wait']}},requiresHint:'Your animation thread needs next_costume(), key_pressed(), and wait() — check the code from Step 5.'},{title:'Step 7 — Run your game!',text:'Click the green flag to start. Use the left and right arrow keys to move. The sprite should animate when moving and show its idle pose when still — both threads running in parallel. 🎉',interactive:true,highlight:'green-flag',highlightLabel:'Click to run!'},{title:'Challenge 1 — Add vertical movement',text:'Add up/down movement to your movement thread. Inside the while True:, after the left/right checks, add:

if key_pressed("up"):
    change_y(5)
if key_pressed("down"):
    change_y(-5)

Also update the animation thread to check for up/down too.',interactive:true,highlight:'threads'},{title:'Challenge 2 — Adjust animation speed',text:'Switch to your animation thread and experiment with the wait() value:

🐇 wait(0.05) — 20fps (faster)
🐢 wait(0.25) — 4fps (slower)

What looks best for your sprite? When happy, click Finish ✓.',interactive:true,highlight:'editor'}]});

quizQuestions.q: Why does putting animation and movement in the same loop cause problems?
options: The wait() slows the whole loop to 10 times per second — including key checks
PyScratch can only define one game_start() function
The while True loop uses too much memory when there is a wait() inside
next_costume() and change_x() cannot be called in the same function

quizQuestions.q: If the animation thread calls wait(0.1), what happens to the movement thread?
options: The movement thread carries on running — wait() only pauses the thread it is called in
The movement thread also waits 0.1 seconds before the next key check
The movement thread stops permanently until animation finishes
Both threads restart from the beginning of game_start()

quizQuestions.q: In the animation thread, why do we check key_pressed() before calling next_costume()?
options: So the costume only animates when the sprite is actually moving
Because next_costume() does not work without a condition
To stop the sprite from bouncing off the edges
Because wait() requires a condition before it can be called

quizQuestions.q: What does set_costume(1) do in the animation thread's else branch?
options: Returns the sprite to its first (idle) costume when no key is held
Deletes all costumes except the first one
Speeds up the animation when the sprite is not moving
Stops the animation thread from running

quizQuestions.q: Which of these correctly completes the animation thread?
options: while True: / if key_pressed('right') or key_pressed('left'): next_costume() / else: set_costume(1) / wait(0.1)
while True: / next_costume() / wait(0.1)
def animation(): / next_costume() / wait(0.1)
while True: / next_costume() / if not key_pressed('right'): wait(0.1)
